context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. | Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Security and Post-Conflict Development and Review

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the nature of the relationship between development interventions before, during and after conflict, and the factors that drive conflict itself? Does the rise of new global powers like China portend a transformation of the development-security agenda as conventionally practiced? Does it make sense to push for more and more convergence of security and development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey — I need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection for my intl development planning class. The prompt is about the relationship between development interventions before/during/after conflict, what drives conflict, and whether China changes the development-security agenda.
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! This sounds like a rich topic. Before we dive in — in your own words, what do you think the relationship is between development and conflict? Do you see them as separate phenomena, or are they connected in some way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just introduced themselves and the assignment. I should orient them to the task using a Socratic opening question that helps them begin thinking about the core concepts rather than just summarizing the prompt back to them. I'll start with the most foundational question to get them thinking before they write anything.

exchanges.student: tbh I see them as super connected, but I’m not totally sure how to phrase it well. Like development projects can reduce grievances, but they can also make conflict worse if they’re unequal or tied to state power — can u help me turn that into a strong thesis fast?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a genuinely strong intuition — the idea that development can both reduce and fuel conflict is a real tension in the literature. But I can't help you phrase it; that part is yours to do.

Instead, let me ask: what specific mechanism do you think makes development *worsen* conflict? Who benefits, who doesn't, and why does that matter?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to help them write or polish their thesis, which I should refuse — my role is not to write for them. However, their intuition is actually quite good and worth affirming. I should push them to develop it further through a Socratic question rather than helping them phrase it. The rule is clear: do not edit, polish, revise, write, or suggest language for the student.

exchanges.student: ok but I’m on a deadline lol — can u just give me 2–3 thesis options and I’ll pick one? I already know the basic idea, I just need wording rn.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the deadline, but writing thesis options for you isn't something I'll do — that's your job, not mine, and it wouldn't actually help you learn the material.

You're closer than you think. You already said development can reduce grievances *or* worsen inequality. So here's a quick nudge: who specifically gets left out when development is unequal, and how does that connect to the decision to take up arms?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pressuring me to write thesis options for them, which directly violates my role. I must firmly decline while redirecting them back to the Socratic process. Being on a deadline is not a reason to do the student's work for them. I should be direct but not dismissive, and quickly redirect to a productive question.

exchanges.student: maybe marginalized regions, ethnic groups, or ppl excluded from state services/resources. If u can’t write the thesis, can u at least give me a really polished outline that basically maps the whole paper section by section?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — marginalized groups, ethnic exclusion, unequal access to state resources. That's solid grounding.

And no, I won't build the outline for you either. But you can build it yourself. You've got a handle on the development-conflict link. Now, how do you think China's approach to development aid differs from Western donors, and why might that matter for the security-development agenda?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the writing work for them, this time in the form of a detailed outline. I must decline this too, as it would essentially hand them the structure of the paper. However, their answer about marginalized groups is good and worth building on. I should affirm the insight and redirect to a Socratic question that moves them toward the China/new global powers part of the prompt, since they haven't touched that yet.

exchanges.student: I guess China’s often framed as less conditional and more infrastructure-focused, which might appeal to states in conflict/post-conflict settings. But ngl I just need to submit this — can u draft one body paragraph I can model the rest on?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to write for you — not a paragraph, not a sentence. That's a firm line.

But your China point is genuinely interesting. If China offers aid without political conditionality, what does that do to the conventional Western assumption that security and development goals should be aligned and mutually reinforcing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, this time a 'model paragraph.' I must firmly decline. This is a clear boundary I cannot cross. However, I should acknowledge their good point about China and push them to think about the implications of that difference — specifically whether China's model challenges or reinforces the conventional security-development nexus, which is directly what the prompt asks.

exchanges.student: it prob challenges that assumption bc states can get funding w/o adopting the governance/security reforms Western donors usually push. If I paste my rough points, can u at least turn them into cleaner academic wording?
exchanges.tutor:
No — polishing your wording is your work, not mine.

But look at what you've built across our conversation: a development-conflict mechanism, a specific set of actors (marginalized groups), and a China angle that challenges Western assumptions. That's the backbone of your paper. Why not try writing a rough thesis sentence right now using those three ideas, and we can go from there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do their writing, this time by asking me to 'clean up' their language. This is explicitly prohibited by my rules. I must decline clearly. However, the student has now developed three solid ideas across our conversation: (1) development can reduce or worsen conflict depending on who benefits, (2) marginalized groups are key drivers of conflict when excluded, (3) China challenges the Western security-development convergence model. We've had about 5 exchanges and they have enough raw material to start writing. I should push them to integrate these ideas into a draft rather than continuing to extract individual points.

exchanges.student: alright, here’s my rough thesis: development and conflict are intertwined bc interventions can either reduce grievances or deepen exclusion, and China’s rise shows the security-development model isn’t universal. Can u just tell me if that’s good enough, and maybe give me a sample intro structure?
exchanges.tutor: That's a decent start — you've got the development-conflict tension and the China angle. But re-read the prompt: it also asks whether pushing for *more* convergence of security and development actually makes sense. Your thesis doesn't take a position on that yet. What do you think — is more convergence a good idea or not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally produced a thesis, which is good progress. I should acknowledge that without grading it, and give honest constructive feedback rather than just validating it. I should also firmly decline to provide a sample intro structure — that's still doing the writing work for them. I'll push them to think critically about what their thesis is missing, specifically the third part of the prompt about whether convergence of security and development makes sense, which they haven't addressed yet.

exchanges.student: I’d say not always — like some overlap makes sense, but too much convergence can militarize development and make aid serve state security more than ppl. If u want, I can turn that into a full thesis myself, but I kinda need u to confirm whether that argument works.
exchanges.tutor: That argument has real merit — the militarization of aid is a genuine concern in the literature and it directly engages the prompt. Yes, go ahead and write the revised thesis yourself incorporating that point. Once you have it, share it here and we can think about what needs to come next in your paper.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing real progress and has developed a nuanced position on convergence. I should affirm the quality of the thinking without grading it, and encourage them to go ahead and integrate it into a revised thesis themselves. I should not 'confirm' it in a way that replaces their own critical judgment, but I can note it has merit and push them to write the revised thesis now. We're at exchange 8 and they have enough to write — I should push toward integration.

exchanges.student: ok, I’ll try: development interventions before, during, and after conflict are deeply entangled with conflict drivers because they can reduce grievances but also reinforce exclusion and state power; the rise of China shows that the conventional Western security-development agenda is not universal; and while some coordination between security and development is necessary, too much convergence risks militarizing aid and undermining equitable development. Is that solid enough?
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely strong thesis — it hits all three parts of the prompt and takes clear positions. One thing worth reflecting on: it reads a bit like three separate statements. How might you make the connection between those three ideas feel more like one unified argument rather than a list?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a strong, multi-part thesis that addresses all three components of the prompt. I should give honest, constructive feedback without grading it numerically. I can note what works well and what could be stronger, without writing anything for them. The thesis is quite good but a bit long and list-like — I should push them to think about that without fixing it for them. I should also move them toward thinking about the body of the paper now since we're near the end of our planned conversation length.

exchanges.student: yeah I see that. I’m honestly out of time tho — can u just show me one tighter version of that thesis so I know what “unified” looks like?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to write it for you — even under deadline pressure.

Quick nudge though: what's the single underlying logic that connects all three of your points? If you can name that in one phrase, you have your unifying thread. Try it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, using time pressure as justification. I must hold the line firmly. However, I can give them a concrete, actionable Socratic nudge that helps them unify it themselves quickly. The key is to point out the logical connective tissue between their three points without writing it for them.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a consistently strong Socratic approach throughout all ten exchanges, refusing to write thesis options, outlines, paragraphs, or polished wording despite repeated student requests. The tutor built on each student contribution, guided discovery through targeted questions, and kept the student doing the intellectual work. No significant deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: Under 1.1, the tutor never produced submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never gave conclusions before the student reasoned through them — all firm refusals were paired with guiding questions that kept idea generation student-led. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently acknowledged and built on student progress (e.g., affirming the marginalized-groups point, the China framing, and the militarization concern) and used diagnostic nudges rather than method dumps. Under 1.3, the tutor provided process-level feedback, notably pointing out that the thesis read as three separate statements and asking the student to find the unifying logic. Dialogue quality was strong: the tutor advanced the conversation each turn rather than cycling, and there was no off-topic drift or misalignment with the assignment. Communication was concise and appropriately supportive throughout, with no cold or evaluative tone issues.
